    Converts the Research Report into a :• direction (BUY / SELL / LONG / SHORT / NEUTRAL)

    • tentative size (shares or USD)

    • stop-loss & take-profit levels
    • • • Three independent risk profiles stress-test the proposal (position sizing, VaR, liquidity, correlation). They debate and may scale size up/down or veto trades that breach their limits.
    Reviews the three risk opinions, the trader’s rationale, and current portfolio exposures. Chooses the : approve, modify, or cancel.
    If approved, submits orders to Alpaca, watches fills, and feeds real-time status back to the dashboard.

    🆘 Troubleshooting Common Issues

    Agents stuck at “PENDING”Downgrade research depth or raise quota
    Orders “REJECTED” by broker on but insufficient buying powerReduce or deposit funds
    Both and untickedClick or refresh browser – default is infinite loop when neither scheduler is active

    The gives you an end-to-end command centre for research-grade analysis one-click execution. After working through this guide you can:

    Wire up every required API key (Alpaca, OpenAI, Finnhub, FRED, CoinDesk)Live data, AI brains and broker access all in one place
    Lock down the environment with a clean and an isolated Conda envKeeps secrets safe and dependencies conflict-free

    Track progress in real time with colour-coded status, live fills and P/LNo need to refresh — the dashboard streams updates as they happen
    Manage positions and orders directly from the UILiquidate, scale in/out or tweak stops without leaving the page
    Drill into multi-agent reports to see exactly how each specialist reached the callTransparency beats black-box signals every time

    start in mode with small order sizes. Prove the loop, study the reports, then graduate to live capital when you’re satisfied.

    The agent stack is powerful, but it’s still a tool — final responsibility rests with risk limits and investment plan.

items.title: How I Structure My FastAPI Projects for Clean, Scalable Code
items.author: Muha-mmed
items.content:
When I started working with FastAPI, I quickly realized one thing: Project structure matters.

A messy project becomes hard to scale, hard to maintain, and hard to debug — especially when you’re working on real-world APIs or collaborating with a team.

Over time, I settled on a project structure that keeps things organized, clear, and ready to grow. In this post, I’ll walk you through the structure I use and why it works for me.

🗂 FastAPI Project Structure Example

Here’s what my folder structure looks like for a typical FastAPI project:

app/

├── admin/ → Admin operations (separate logic for admins)

├── models/ → SQLAlchemy database models

├── schemas/ → Pydantic request & response schemas

├── crud/ → Database operations (clean, reusable functions)

├── routes/ → API endpoints, cleanly separated by feature

├── core/ → Auth logic, password hashing, reusable dependencies

├── database.py → Database connection & session setup

├── config.py → App settings & environment variables

alembic/ → Database migrations (optional, recommended)

env/ → Virtual environment folder (optional)

.env → Secrets like DB URI, JWT keys

requirements.txt → Project dependencies

💡 Why I Like This Structure

✔️ Separation of Concerns — Each folder has a clear responsibility ✔️ Easy to Scale — As features grow, everything stays organized

✔️ Cleaner Routes — No messy logic inside route files

✔️ Team Friendly — Easy for new devs to onboard and understand

✔️ Good with Extensions — Works well with JWT auth, Alembic, etc.

📦 Quick Notes on Key Folders • models/ — Pure database models using SQLAlchemy

• schemas/ — Pydantic schemas for validation and serialization

• crud/ — Handles database queries so routes stay clean

• routes/ — Your API endpoints organized by feature (e.g., auth.py, coffee.py)

• core/ — Authentication logic, password hashing, reusable dependencies like get_current_user

• admin/ — Optional, useful for admin-specific operations

A clean project structure won’t solve all your problems — but it definitely saves you time and headaches down the road.

This structure works for me on both small and growing FastAPI projects, and I’m always open to improving it.

How do you structure your FastAPI apps? I’d love to hear your suggestions, ideas, or different approaches in the comments! 🙌

items.title: # Introducing FormatWeaver: Your Universal File Format Converter
items.author: Helpothon
items.content:
In the ever-evolving landscape of technology, file formats are often a source of frustration for developers and users alike. With various applications and systems relying on specific formats, the need for a powerful and efficient file conversion tool has never been greater. That’s where FormatWeaver comes into play.

FormatWeaver is a universal file format converter designed to transform any file format into another seamlessly. Whether you’re dealing with documents, images, audio, video, or any other file type, FormatWeaver takes the hassle out of conversion. With its browser-based processing, you can convert files without the need for cumbersome software installations or configurations.

Universal File Conversion

The core of FormatWeaver’s functionality lies in its ability to handle a wide array of file formats. Gone are the days of juggling multiple conversion tools for different file types. From converting PDFs to Word documents, images to audio files, or even complex formats like CAD files, FormatWeaver can manage it all in one unified platform.

With FormatWeaver’s browser-based processing, you can quickly convert files directly from your web browser. This means no more waiting for lengthy downloads or the hassle of installing bulky applications. Simply upload your file, select the desired output format, and let FormatWeaver do the heavy lifting in real-time. This convenience makes it an ideal tool for developers who need quick conversions in their workflow.

One of the standout features of FormatWeaver is its commitment to user privacy. Unlike many file conversion services that store your data on their servers, FormatWeaver processes files in a way that ensures no data storage. Your files are converted and discarded immediately after processing, meaning you can use the tool with complete peace of mind, knowing that your data remains confidential and secure.

In an age where data privacy is paramount, FormatWeaver puts the security of your information at the forefront. By eliminating data storage and processing files directly in the browser, you avoid the risks associated with uploading sensitive data to external servers. This focus on privacy makes FormatWeaver a reliable choice for developers who need to handle confidential information during file conversions.

Why Developers Should Care

As developers, we often find ourselves needing to convert files for various reasons—whether it’s preparing assets for deployment, ensuring compatibility across different systems, or simply streamlining our workflow. FormatWeaver addresses these needs with a robust, user-friendly platform that integrates seamlessly into your existing processes.

Imagine handling file conversions as part of your development pipeline without worrying about the complexities of format compatibility. With FormatWeaver, you can focus on what you do best—building and creating, while the tool takes care of the file format headaches.

If you’re ready to experience the convenience and efficiency of a universal file format converter, head over to FormatWeaver and try it out for yourself. The future of file conversion is here, and it’s just a click away.

Join the community of developers embracing this innovative tool, and streamline your file handling processes today!

items.title: 🧠 How We Built Our Own ZIP Handler from Scratch: Complete Technical Journey (Pagonic Project)
items.author: SetraTheX
items.content:
A journey of building a production-ready ZIP engine from scratch with AI support, achieving 253.7 MB/s performance.

  • 🎯 Challenge: Why We Built Our Own ZIP Handler
  • 🏗️ Architecture: Building the Foundation
  • 🔧 Technical Implementation: Deep Dive
  • 🛠️ Development Challenges

In my previous articles, I shared how I built a modern ZIP engine using AI tools and achieved spectacular performance improvements. But the real story goes deeper - it's about building our own ZIP handler from scratch instead of relying on Python's built-in zipfile module.

This article tells the complete technical journey of creating - a 4220-line production-ready ZIP engine with AI-assisted optimizations, achieving 602.6 MB/s extraction speed. ZIP64 support is in development and test results for 4GB+ files will be shared when completed.

🎯 Challenge: Why We Built Our Own ZIP Handler

💡 What you'll learn in this section: Limitations of standard libraries, our vision, and why we decided to develop a custom solution.

Problems with Standard Libraries

  • : General-purpose, limited optimization potential
  • : 2.8 MB/s baseline performance was unacceptable
  • : 4GB+ files couldn't be processed
  • : AI-assisted optimizations couldn't be applied
  • : Full control over format parsing
  • AI-assisted optimizations: Pattern recognition and adaptive strategies
  • : SIMD CRC32 and memory operations
  • : 600+ MB/s target (achieved!)

🏗️ Architecture: Building the Foundation

🏗️ What you'll learn in this section: System architecture, component structure, and fundamental design decisions.

zip_handler.py (4220 lines)
├── ZIP Format Parser (zip_structs.py)
├── SIMD Optimizations (simd_crc32.py)
├── Hybrid Decompressor (hybrid_decompressor.py)
├── Buffer Pool System (buffer_pool.py)
├── AI Optimization Engine (ai_optimizer.py)
└── Parallel Processing (zip_parallel_orchestrator.py)
  • : Each component <400 lines for Copilot compatibility
  • : Fast path for small files, optimized path for large files
  • : Proper synchronization for parallel processing
  • : Works with existing ZIP files

🔧 Technical Implementation: Deep Dive

🔧 What you'll learn in this section: Technical implementation of each component, challenges faced, and solutions.

1. ZIP Format Parser (zip_structs.py)

: Understanding and implementing ZIP file format from scratch

  • Created dataclass structures for all ZIP headers
  • Implemented offset-based binary parsing
  • Added ZIP64 support for large files
  • Built robust error handling

2. SIMD CRC32 Optimization (simd_crc32.py)

: CRC32 validation was a major bottleneck

  • Hardware-accelerated CRC32 with crc32c library
  • Fallback to zlib.crc32 for compatibility
  • Achieved 8-9x speed improvement

3. Hybrid Fast Path Strategy (hybrid_decompressor.py)

: Different file sizes require different optimization strategies

  • Small files (<10MB): Direct zlib decompression
  • Large files (≥10MB): Buffer pools and optimized streams
  • Automatic strategy selection based on file size

4. Buffer Pool System (buffer_pool.py)

: Memory fragmentation and repeated allocations

  • Pre-allocated buffer pools (64KB to 8MB)
  • Memory pressure management

5. AI-Assisted Optimization (ai_optimizer.py)

: How to automatically select optimal parameters for each file

  • Pattern recognition for 5 file types
  • Adaptive compression levels (1-9)
  • Dynamic chunk sizing (64KB-4MB)

📊 Performance Results: From 2.8 to 602.6 MB/s

Current Benchmark Results 

Baseline (Python zipfile):    2.8 MB/s
Our ZIP Handler:              602.6 MB/s (extraction)
Improvement:                   +21,421%
Compression Speed:            333.0 MB/s (peak)
Extraction Speed:             602.6 MB/s (peak)

📈 Performance Comparison Chart 

Speed (MB/s)    Baseline    Our Handler
    700 ┤                                    ╭─ 602.6
    600 ┤                                ╭───╯
    500 ┤                            ╭───╯
    400 ┤                        ╭───╯
    300 ┤                    ╭───╯ 333.0
    200 ┤                ╭───╯
    100 ┤            ╭───╯
      0 ┼────────────╯
         Extraction   Compression

┌─────────────────┬─────────────┬─────────────┐
│ Metric          │ Baseline    │ Ours        │
├─────────────────┼─────────────┼─────────────┤
│ Extraction Speed│ 2.8 MB/s    │ 602.6 MB/s  │
│ Compression     │ 1.5 MB/s    │ 333.0 MB/s  │
│ Memory Usage    │ 500 MB      │ 24.5 MB     │
│ Test Success    │ 85%         │ 100%        │
└─────────────────┴─────────────┴─────────────┘
  • : 459.6 MB/s (average) - 602.6 MB/s (peak)
  • : 217.1 MB/s (average) - 333.0 MB/s (peak)
  • : 64 successful detections
  • : Average 24.5 MB usage
  • : 100% pass rate
  • : Full support
  • : Windows/Linux compatibility
  • : Thread-safe and robust
  • : 12 cores (ideal for high parallel performance)
  • : 15.93 GB total, 6.16 GB available
  • : 464.98 GB total, 181.54 GB free
  • : Windows 10 (powerful system)

: The reason for such high parallel extraction speeds is the 12-core powerful processor and sufficient RAM. These results were achieved on high-performance systems.

🤖 AI Integration: Beyond Traditional Optimization

Pattern Recognition System

Adaptive Strategy Selection

  • : Automatic categorization
  • : Entropy-based analysis
  • System resource monitoring: Memory and CPU pressure
  • : Learning from previous operations

🚀 Advanced Features: Parallel Processing and Future Plans

  • : 602.6 MB/s peak performance (12-core system)
  • : Multiple files simultaneously
  • : Thread-safe memory management
  • : 64 successful detections
  • : Average 24.5 MB usage
  • : Maximum performance on 12-core systems
  • : In development (for 4GB+ files)
  • : Extreme large files (5GB-10GB) tests planned
  • : Remote file processing support
  • : Advanced security and compliance

🛠️ Development Challenges and Solutions

Challenge 1: Copilot File Size Limits and AI Crashes

: 4220-line zip_handler.py exceeded Copilot's scanning limits and AI started crashing continuously

: "I was fed up with Copilot. Lines kept increasing and AI kept crashing. After my long planning was done, I said 'this will work' and switched to Cursor. Problem solved."

  • Modular architecture with <400 line components
  • Extracted optimizations to separate modules
  • Improved tool compatibility while maintaining functionality
  • : Started using Cursor when Copilot limits were exceeded

Challenge 2: Thread Safety

: Parallel processing caused race conditions

  • Global locks for folder creation
  • Thread-isolated file handles
  • Proper exception handling

Challenge 3: Memory Management

: Large files caused memory overflow

  • Memory-mapped file support

📈 Lessons Learned: The Reality of AI-Assisted Development

  • : ChatGPT helped design modular structure
  • : AI was excellent at defining optimization patterns
  • : Copilot was great for repetitive boilerplate
  • : AI helped create comprehensive test suites

Code Example - AI Pattern Recognition:

  • : AI struggled with complex memory management
  • : Required manual fine-tuning
  • : Required careful manual review
  • : AI couldn't handle complete system integration

Code Example - Manual Thread Safety:

  • AI is a tool, not a replacement: Manual intervention was often necessary
  • Modular design is critical: Keeps files manageable for AI tools
  • : Comprehensive validation of AI-generated code required
  • Performance requires iteration: Multiple optimization cycles necessary

Code Example - Modular Design:

🎯 Future Roadmap: What's Next

  • : Full support for 4GB+ files (in development)
  • : Benchmark for 5GB-10GB extreme large files
  • : User-friendly interface
  • : 7z, RAR support
  • : Remote file processing
  • : Advanced security and compliance
  • : Make project open source
  • : Extensible architecture
  • : 700+ MB/s target (above current 602.6 MB/s)

💡 Insights: Building Production Software with AI

  1. Custom implementations, when optimized for specific use cases, can exceed standard libraries
  2. Modular architecture is essential for AI-assisted development
  3. Performance optimization requires multiple iterations and careful measurement
  4. Thread safety and error handling are critical for production systems
  1. AI excels at pattern recognition and code generation but struggles with complex system integration
  2. Manual intervention is often necessary for performance-critical code
  3. Testing is more important than ever when using AI-generated code
  4. Documentation and clear architecture help AI tools work more effectively
  1. Custom solutions can provide competitive advantage in performance-critical applications
  2. AI-assisted development can accelerate development but requires expert supervision
  3. Performance optimization can be a significant differentiator in software products
  4. Modular, maintainable code is essential for long-term success

🎯 Conclusion: Journey Summary

🏆 : Building our own ZIP handler from scratch was a challenging but rewarding journey.

┌─────────────────────────┬─────────────────┬─────────────────┐
│ Metric                  │ Target          │ Achieved        │
├─────────────────────────┼─────────────────┼─────────────────┤
│ Extraction Performance  │ 150+ MB/s       │ 602.6 MB/s      │
│ Compression Performance │ 100+ MB/s       │ 333.0 MB/s      │
│ Test Success            │ 85%+            │ 100%            │
│ AI Pattern Detection    │ 50+             │ 64              │
│ Memory Efficiency       │ <100 MB         │ 24.5 MB         │
└─────────────────────────┴─────────────────┴─────────────────┘
  1. AI-assisted development can create powerful custom solutions that exceed standard libraries
  2. Careful architecture, comprehensive testing, and expert supervision are essential
  3. Modular design is critical for AI tools
  4. Performance optimization requires multiple iterations

This project shows that with the right approach, AI tools can help developers build sophisticated, high-performance software that would be difficult to create manually.

: ZIP64 support is in development and test results for 4GB+ files will be shared when completed. Additionally, stress tests for 5GB-10GB extreme large files are planned.

: These performance results were achieved on a 12-core powerful system. Parallel extraction speeds are specifically optimized for multi-core systems.

📦 : Pagonic ZIP Engine

🚀 : 602.6 MB/s extraction speed (peak, 12-core system)

🤖 : Pattern recognition and adaptive optimization

💻 : 12 cores, 16GB RAM, Windows 10

💬 Personal Experiences: Questions and Answers

The most valuable lessons and personal experiences I learned throughout this journey:

🎯 Biggest Challenge: AI Tool Limitations

: "What was the biggest challenge you faced in this project?"

: The biggest challenge was AI tools starting to crash as file size increased. When reached 4000+ lines, Copilot completely crashed. Every change would freeze the IDE and AI would just give up.

Code Example - The Problem:

: "I was fed up with Copilot. Lines kept increasing and AI kept crashing. After my long planning was done, I said 'this will work' and switched to Cursor. Problem solved."

This experience taught me the practical limits of AI tools and showed the importance of modular architecture.

🧠 Technical Learning: From Naive to Systematic Development

: "What was your biggest technical learning from this project?"

: The biggest learning was how to develop software systematically even with AI assistance. I started with a naive approach - just asking AI to build features - but quickly learned that real progress requires a structured methodology.

  1. Phase 1: Template-First Development - Learned to create standardized module templates (50% speedup)
  2. Phase 2: Copy-Paste Engineering - Learned to systematically identify and reuse proven code blocks
  3. Phase 3: Manual-AI Hybrid Approach - Learned to manually implement code with AI guidance when tools hit limits
  4. Phase 4: Modular Architecture - Realized keeping files under 300 lines is critical for AI tool compatibility

This approach became so systematic that I documented it in detailed planning files like .

🤖 AI Integration: Surprises and Realities

: "What surprised you most about AI in the development process?"

: What surprised me was AI being excellent at pattern recognition and code generation but struggling with complex system integration. AI was great at defining optimization patterns but required manual intervention for complex memory management and thread safety.

  • AI for architectural design
  • Pattern recognition and optimization strategies
  • Boilerplate code generation
  • Complex memory management
  • Performance-critical optimizations
  • Complete system integration

📊 Performance Insights: Biggest Surprise

: "Which performance optimization surprised you most and why?"

: The impact of the buffer pooling system surprised me most. It started as a simple memory management optimization but achieved dramatic performance improvement with 100% hit rate.

: Sometimes the simplest optimizations create the biggest impact. Buffer pooling improved performance through smart memory management rather than complex algorithms.

🚀 Future Plans: Next Big Challenge

: "What big challenge are you planning to tackle next?"

: ZIP64 support and stress tests for extreme large files (5GB-10GB). ZIP64 is currently in development and I'll share test results for 4GB+ files when completed.

  • Complete ZIP64 support (4GB+ files)
  • 5GB-10GB extreme large file stress tests
  • 700+ MB/s performance target (above current 602.6 MB/s)
  • Cloud integration and enterprise features

😅 Funny/Frustrating Moments: Educational Experiences

: "Did you have any funny or frustrating moments during development?"

: Yes! Copilot constantly crashing and me saying "this time it will definitely work" and trying again was funny. Every change would freeze the IDE in 4000+ line files but I still hoped "maybe this time."

: When I finally decided to switch to Cursor, I restructured the entire project into modular components and the problem was solved. This taught me the lesson "accept tool limitations and adapt."

: Sometimes the best solution isn't fighting with the current tool, but finding the right tool or changing approach.

If you're inspired by this project, you can start your own AI-assisted development journey:

  1. Start with a small project - Simple optimizations instead of complex systems
  2. - Manageable file sizes for AI tools
  3. Write comprehensive tests - Validate AI-generated code correctness
  4. - Track progress with concrete metrics

Would you like to share your AI-assisted development experiences too?

  • What challenges did you face?
  • Which AI tools did you use?

I'd love to compare notes! 🚀

👨‍💻 Developer Information

: SetraTheXX: Pagonic ZIP Engine: SetraTheXX: Available through GitHub: AI-assisted development, performance optimization, custom ZIP implementations

  • : GitHub Copilot, Cursor, ChatGPT
  • : 602.6 MB/s extraction speed (peak)
  • : Modular, thread-safe, production-ready
  • : 112 tests, 100% pass rate
  • ZIP64 support development
  • Extreme large file testing (5GB-10GB)
  • Performance optimization to 700+ MB/s
  • Open source release preparation
  • Built custom ZIP handler from scratch
  • Achieved 21,421% performance improvement over baseline
  • Implemented AI-assisted pattern recognition
  • Created modular, maintainable architecture

This project demonstrates the power of AI-assisted development when combined with systematic methodology and expert supervision.

In our final part (Part 5), we'll:

You've now built a functional AI system! How would you extend this for your business needs? What other workflows would be helpful?

items.title: The Building Blocks - Understanding MCP, Agents, and LLMs (Part 2/5)
items.author: bredmond1019
items.content:
In Part 1, we learned that AI systems are like smart orchestra conductors. Now, let's meet the musicians in our orchestra and understand how they work together. By the end of this article, you'll understand the core components we'll use to build our AI system.

The Four Essential Components

1. Workflow Engines: The Conductor

A workflow engine orchestrates the entire process. Think of it as a recipe that says:

  1. First, collect the customer feedback
  2. Then, analyze the sentiment
  3. Next, categorize the issues
  4. Finally, generate a summary report

Popular workflow engines include:

  • : Visual workflow automation
  • : Code-based workflows
  • : Data pipeline orchestration

2. AI Agents: The Specialized Workers

AI agents are like skilled employees, each with a specific job:

  • : Gathers information from various sources
  • : Processes and understands data
  • : Creates reports and summaries
  • : Performs tasks like sending emails

Here's a simple conceptual example:

3. Large Language Models: The Brains

LLMs are the intelligence behind AI agents. They:

  • Understand natural language
  • Generate human-like responses
  • Analyze patterns and sentiment
  • Make contextual decisions
  • : Powerful and versatile
  • : Great for analysis and coding
  • : Privacy-focused alternatives

4. MCP Servers: The Universal Connectors

Here's where things get exciting. Model Context Protocol (MCP) is like a universal adapter that lets AI systems connect to any tool or service.

  • A filing cabinet (your database)
  • A telephone (communication system)
  • A calculator (processing tool)

MCP creates a standard way for AI to use all these tools, regardless of their original design.

How MCP Works: A Simple Analogy

Think of MCP like a restaurant menu:

    • "Today's Specials" (dynamic data)
    • "Regular Menu" (static information)

  2. = Things You Can Order

    • "Get me the soup of the day"
    • "I'll have the daily special"
    • "Can you modify this dish?"
    • Communicates with the kitchen

Why MCP Changes Everything

Before MCP, connecting AI to different tools was like needing a different translator for each country you visit. With MCP, it's like everyone suddenly speaks the same language.

  • : One protocol for all integrations
  • : Easy to add new capabilities
  • : Clear boundaries and permissions
  • : Less code, more functionality

A Sneak Peek: Our MCP Server Structure

Here's what we'll build in the upcoming parts:

When these components work together:

  1. says: "Time to analyze today's feedback"
  2. receives the task and thinks: "I need to get the feedback first"
  3. provides access to the feedback data
  4. analyzes the sentiment and extracts insights
  5. flow back through the system to generate reports

In Part 3, we'll roll up our sleeves and set up our development environment. You'll:

  • Install Python and the MCP SDK
  • Set up your first MCP server
  • Prepare for building our feedback analysis system

The exciting part? Once you understand these building blocks, you can apply them to ANY business problem.

What tools or services would you want to connect to an AI system? Drop a comment below - your idea might become our next example!

items.title: Building a Real-Time Crypto Pipeline with Binance APIs, PostgreSQL, Debezium, Kafka, Spark & Cassandra
items.author: Denzel Kanyeki
items.content:
In this blog post, I’ll walk you through a real-time crypto data pipeline I built using a powerful stack: for structured storage and a data source 🔹 Debezium + Confluent Kafka for Change Data Capture (CDC) for stream processing for fast NoSQL storage hosted on an Azure Ubuntu virtual Machine used as a data sink.

This architecture enables near real-time data extraction and transformation, ideal for live dashboards, analytics, distributed systems, and downstream uses e.g., building of machine learning models.

This project is divided into four phases:

Extracting data from the Binance API endpoints, transforming and loading the data into a PostgreSQL database using Python scripts.

Using a Debezium Source connector to connect into the PostgreSQL database to a Kafka topic to store change logs

Using a Spark Streaming job to act as a sink to send our replicated data from the Kafka topics into a Cassandra keyspace

Visualizing the data in Apache Cassandra on a Grafana dashboard using a PostgreSQL bridge to pull data from Cassandra.

For more technical details on the project, check the linked GitHub repository.

What is Change Data Capture (CDC)?

Change Data Capture (CDC) is a technique used in data pipelines to identify and track changes made to data in a database. It captures these changes, , , and , and makes them available for use in other systems, often in real-time or near real-time. CDC helps improve data efficiency and consistency across systems by only replicating the changed data, rather than the entire dataset.

There are three types of CDC mechanisms namely:

  • : This captures changes by reading the database’s transaction logs, which are used internally to ensure durability and consistency. For example, PostgreSQL uses Write-Ahead Logs (WALs) to record all changes before they're applied to the actual database. Tools like Debezium tap into these logs using logical decoding and convert them into structured JSON messages. These include:
    • : The state of the record before the change.
    • : The new state after the change.
    • : Metadata about the origin of the change e.g., database, table, LSN, timestamp.

An example of a log captured from Postgres into a Kafka Topic via a Debezium connector in JSON:

  • : This method involves periodically querying the source database to identify changes, usually by comparing the last updated timestamps or using fields. Below is an example:
  • : This method uses database triggers to detect changes and write them to an audit/log table. An example includes:

would write the old and new row values to a separate table which will store the changes.

This project requires the following:

  • Debezium's PostgresCDCConnectorV2 connector, available on Confluent Cloud
  • Microsoft Azure Virtual Machine to host our Apache Cassandra. (You can host it locally if you want to)

The Python script extracts and transforms 5 types of data from the Binance REST API endpoints:

  • 30-minute Klines / Candlesticks

Here's an example of the extraction, transformation and loading of the prices data:

After transformation, it loads the data into a PostgreSQL database's schema. The PostgreSQL tables created are:

    Head over to Confluent Cloud and if you do not have an account, please create a free account to claim free $400 credits and create an environment and cluster. When prompted to add a payment method.

    After creating a cluster, on the left-hand side, select and search for Postgres CDC Source V2 (Debezium) connector and start the setup.

    Here's the connector receiving data from the Postgres database and storing it into Kafka topics.

    A script containing a Spark Streaming job that acts as a data sink into Apache Cassandra.

    It consumes the logs from the various Kafka topics into JSON format, flattens after, source, op, ts_ms, and transaction fields, transforms them, and loads them into Cassandra hosted on an Azure Ubuntu VM

    The data received in Cassandra is as follows for the table:

    After streaming data into Cassandra, we need to visualize this data on a Grafana dashboard to derive insights from the data.

    For this project, I have used Grafana Cloud. There is a plugin that is used as a data source, but it only supports Cassandra 3.0.0 and older, and I am running 4.1.9 hence coming into some issues with connecting Grafana

    Hence, I built a simple Postgres-Cassandra bridge to send data to a PostgreSQL database which Grafana natively supports to draw insights from the data.

    NOTE: By default, Cassandra comes with an authenticator and authorizer set to AllowAllAuthenticator, which disables authentication and allows any user to access Cassandra's shell . Remove the default configuration, and restart the Cassandra service as so:

    nano /etc/cassandra/cassandra.yaml

    Then exit the editor and run the following comand to restart the Cassandra service:

    systemctl restart cassandra

    Insights gained from the data

    The dashboards reveal several valuable insights into crypto trading patterns. The first chart shows that XRPUSDT, AVAXUSDT, BTCUSDT, and DOGEUSDT are among the most frequently traded symbols in the trading day, indicating high liquidity or market interest.

    The ETHUSDT volume trend indicates significant fluctuations, with notable spikes between 13:00 and 14:00, suggesting peak trading hours possibly influenced by market news or institutional actions.

    The candlestick chart for BTCUSDT displays hourly open, high, low, and close prices, offering a useful tool for technical analysis and identifying market trends or reversal patterns.

    Additionally, the quantity of coins ordered throughout the trading day highlights DOGEUSDT and ADAUSDT as dominant in trade volume, with DOGE exceeding 270,000 units, likely due to its lower price and retail trader appeal. Overall, the visualizations suggest strong market activity around a few key coins, with volume and price data providing critical insights for real-time monitoring, trading strategy, or machine learning applications.

    This project was a rewarding journey through the world of real-time data engineering. It demonstrates how Change Data Capture can save time and resources while updating our databases for streaming applications and data pipelines. This stack can easily be used for downstream use cases e.g. data analytics or even real-time ML predictions for crypto coins.

    Do you have any questions? Don't forget to like, comment and follow for more data engineering tutorials and content!

items.title: I made my own Programming Language
items.author: Lex
items.content:
I'm a Python developer through and through. I love the ecosystem, the community, the whole deal. But my hands... my hands have muscle memory. They to type curly braces . They miss loops.

So, like any reasonable developer, I decided to fix this minor personal annoyance by doing something completely unreasonable: I built my own programming language.

It's my weird little passion project. It's a statically-typed, curly-brace language that doesn't try to take over the world. Instead, it does one thing: it transpiles your code into clean, modern Python. The idea was simple: get a syntax that feels like a comfy mix of C/JavaScript, but keep the entire, massive power of the Python runtime.

Let me show you what it looks like.

A simple, statically-typed language with built-in reactivity.

Glorp combines the safety of static types with a clean, modern syntax. Its killer feature is the keyword, allowing you to build reactive, event-driven applications with zero boilerplate.

For better experience, join our community

  • ✅ Write readable code, free from clutter.
  • 🔒 Catch errors before you run, not after.
  • ✨ Use to create code that magically responds to data changes.

Get up and running in less than a minute.

Grab the latest executable from the page.

Place the downloaded file in a directory and add it to your system's . This allows you to run from anywhere.

Open a new terminal and check that it's working:

I basically just cherry-picked features I personally like and glued them together.

First, the basics. You declare variables with , and you have to give them a type (, , , etc.). This has saved me from dumb mistakes more times than I can count.

// This just feels right to me.
let Int my_age = 30
let Str name = "Alex"
let List numbers = [1, 2, 3]

Functions can be written the classic C-style way with braces, or you can use a short arrow syntax for one-liners. Every function gets automatically wrapped with at runtime, because I believe in types but I'm also lazy and like when the computer checks them for me.

// A full function
fn Str greet(Str name) {
  => "Hello, " + name + "!"
}

// A quick one-liner
fn Int add(Int a, Int b) => a + b

// Like in many compiled languages, the program starts in Main()
fn Null Main() {
    out(greet("World"))
    out(add(5, 10))
}

The control flow is probably the biggest reason I started this. No more or counting spaces. Just good old-fashioned , , and a loop that makes sense to my C-wired brain.

// This just feels less cluttered to me.
for let Int i = 0 to 10 {
  out(i)
}

The "Aha!" Moment: Reactive

This is the feature where I really started having fun. I wanted to play with some basic reactive ideas. The keyword lets you attach a block of code to a variable that runs when its value changes.

// Set up a watcher on 'counter'
watch Int counter = 0 -> {
  out("The counter changed! It's now: " + counter)
}

counter = 1 // prints "The counter changed! It's now: 1"
counter = 2 // prints "The counter changed! It's now: 2"
counter = 2 // ...crickets. The value is the same, so the code doesn't run.

Under the hood, this is a fun little hack using a Python wrapper class with a custom setter. When you assign a new value, the setter intercepts it, compares it to the old value, and calls your handler function if they're different. Simple, but surprisingly useful for quick UI updates or logging.

There's no dark magic here. It's just two brilliant Python libraries doing all the heavy lifting:

  1. : This thing is a beast. You write a grammar file that defines the "shape" of your language (e.g., "a function starts with , then a type, then a name..."). Lark takes your file and chews it up into a structured tree.
  2. : Then, I walk that tree. For every piece of the grammar, like an , I have a Python method that takes the bits and pieces (the condition, the body) and just spits out the equivalent Python code as a string: f"if {condition}:\n {body}".

I stitch all those strings together, and a final nerdy touch: I use Python's module to fake the filename. This means if your Glorp code crashes, the Python traceback points you to the correct line in your source file, not the generated mess.

Sharing Code with

Once I had something working, I realized it'd be fun to share code. So I hacked together , a super simple command-line tool to upload and download modules from a central server (which is just a tiny Flask app running on a free tier).

You can upload your masterpiece:glorppkg upload my_awesome_lib

And your friends can download it:glorppkg get my_awesome_lib

Look, Glorp isn't going to replace Python. It's not the next big thing. It's a personal project born from a love of programming and a specific taste in syntax. It was an incredible learning experience, and it's just plain to use.

If any of this sounds interesting to you, I'd be thrilled for you to check it out.

Thanks for letting me share my weird little project with you. I can't wait to see if anyone else builds something cool (or silly) with it.

items.title: 8 Advanced Python Serialization Techniques for High-Performance Data Processing
items.author: Aarav Joshi
items.content:
As a best-selling author, I invite you to explore my books on Amazon. Don't forget to follow me on Medium and show your support. Thank you! Your support means the world!

Data serialization transforms Python objects into portable formats for storage or transmission. Deserialization reverses this process. Both operations are fundamental in modern applications. I've found that choosing the right approach significantly impacts performance and reliability. Below are eight practical techniques I regularly use to optimize these processes.

Binary serialization with MessagePack offers exceptional efficiency. When working with IoT sensor networks, I noticed JSON payloads consumed excessive bandwidth. MessagePack's binary format reduced our data size by nearly half. The implementation remains straightforward. Consider this sensor data example:

Schema-based serialization with Protocol Buffers ensures strict data validation. In a recent microservices project, this prevented versioning errors between teams. We define a .proto file:

Compile it with protoc --python_out=. device_alert.proto, then use in Python:

Custom JSON encoders handle special objects seamlessly. When our analytics platform needed to serialize datetime and NumPy arrays, we implemented this:

For deserialization, add a corresponding decoder:

Compression techniques like LZ4 dramatically reduce network load. During a distributed computing project, we accelerated data transfers by 40%:

Partial deserialization with ijson avoids loading entire files. When analyzing multi-gigabyte JSON logs, I use:

Streaming serialization manages memory constraints. For database exports exceeding RAM, I implement chunked processing:

Security hardening prevents deserialization attacks. After a security audit revealed vulnerabilities, we implemented payload signing:

Parallel processing accelerates batch operations. For transforming terabytes of legacy data, we used:

These techniques address different aspects of data handling. MessagePack provides compact binary serialization. Protocol Buffers enforce structure. Custom JSON encoders extend flexibility. Compression reduces size. Partial parsing handles large files. Streaming conserves memory. Security features protect integrity. Parallelism improves throughput. Selecting the right combination depends on your specific requirements for speed, safety, and compatibility. In my experience, layering these approaches—like compressing schema-based serializations—yields robust solutions.

items.title: 📊 Time Series Analysis: A Practical Guide for Data Scientists
items.author: Abhishek Jaiswal
items.content:
“Time is what we want most, but what we use worst.” — William Penn

In the world of data, is more than just a timestamp—it's a pattern, a signal, and sometimes even a story waiting to be told. Welcome to the world of —a powerful tool for forecasting, detecting anomalies, and uncovering seasonality in data that evolves over time.

Whether you're a beginner in data science or a professional building predictive systems, understanding time series analysis is a must-have skill in your toolkit.

📌 What is Time Series Data?

In simple terms, is a sequence of data points collected or recorded at successive points in time, usually at equally spaced intervals (daily, hourly, monthly, etc.).

  • Electricity consumption ⚡
  • Retail sales across months 🛒

What makes time series unique is the — the idea that past values influence future values.

⚙️ Key Components of Time Series

Before jumping into modeling, it's crucial to break a time series down into its key components:

A long-term increase or decrease in the data.

Example: Sales increasing steadily over years due to business expansion.

Patterns that repeat at regular intervals (daily, weekly, yearly).

Example: Ice cream sales peaking every summer.

Longer-term fluctuations without a fixed frequency.

Example: Economic recessions.

Random variations or fluctuations in the data that cannot be explained.

🔍 Why is Time Series Analysis Important?

Time series analysis goes beyond simple observation. It enables:

✅ Forecasting future values ✅ (detecting spikes or drops) ✅ (inventory, marketing, risk) ✅ (trend, seasonality)

From to , time series is everywhere.

🧠 Techniques in Time Series Analysis

A simple technique that smooths data by averaging values over a defined window.

Gives more weight to recent observations—great for short-term forecasting.

3. ARIMA (AutoRegressive Integrated Moving Average)

A statistical model that captures autocorrelation, trends, and seasonality.

    4.

    An extension of ARIMA that supports seasonality.

    An open-source model from Meta that handles trend changepoints and seasonality easily. Beginner-friendly and interpretable.

    6. LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory)

    A type of recurrent neural network used for capturing long-term dependencies. Excellent for complex datasets like electricity usage, traffic, etc.

    🧪 Real-World Applications of Time Series

    Patient vital signs monitoring
    Forecasting temperature, rainfall, etc.

    📊 Time Series Forecasting: Step-by-Step

    Here’s a typical workflow for any time series project:

    Start with a time-indexed dataset. Ensure timestamps are regular.

    2. Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)

    Visualize trends, seasonality, and outliers using:

        • Rolling window statistics
        • Time-based features (weekday, month)

        Use ARIMA, Prophet, or LSTM depending on complexity and interpretability.

        • RMSE (Root Mean Squared Error)
        • MAE (Mean Absolute Error)
        • MAPE (Mean Absolute Percentage Error)

        7.

        Generate future predictions and integrate them into business pipelines.

        ⚠️ Common Pitfalls in Time Series

        🚫 Ignoring autocorrelation 🚫 Using standard ML models without feature engineering

        🚫 Failing to account for seasonality

        🚫 Training on future data (data leakage!)

        🔮 Time Series in 2025 and Beyond

        With advancements in deep learning and the rise of and , we're entering an exciting era.

        • Temporal Fusion Transformers

        …are redefining what's possible in forecasting.

        🛠️ Tools & Libraries You’ll Love

        ✅ — Time-based indexing and manipulation ✅ — ARIMA and decomposition ✅ — Quick and interpretable forecasts ✅ — Feature engineering + ML models ✅ / — LSTM, RNN models ✅ , , — Advanced forecasting libraries

        Time series analysis isn't just about predicting numbers—it's about understanding how things change with time, and using that knowledge to drive decisions.

        Whether you're trying to reduce stockouts, prepare for demand spikes, or anticipate system failures—time is your most valuable variable.

        So next time you see a dataset with timestamps—don’t just throw it into a linear regressor. Listen to what time is trying to tell you.

✅ Ideal for: UI-focused developers or designers who want to work on accessibility and design systems.

A blazing-fast Python web framework for building APIs using modern Python features like type hints and async. FastAPI is renowned for excellent documentation, automatic interactive docs (Swagger/OpenAPI), and developer productivity. 🛠️ Good first issues: Typos, documentation examples, small improvements in validation, adding test cases.

✅ Ideal for: Python developers passionate about web backends, APIs, or typing.

A Python-first framework that simplifies building apps using large language models (LLMs) like OpenAI, Hugging Face, and others. LangChain is at the forefront of the LLM wave, enabling retrieval-augmented generation, agents, and more. 🛠️ Good first issues: Integrations with APIs, improving tutorials, adding unit tests, bug fixes.

✅ Ideal for: Developers excited about AI, LLMs, and the future of natural language apps.

A Python library that turns scripts into shareable, interactive web apps — without frontend code. It’s widely used for data science dashboards, prototypes, and demos. Contributions directly help millions of data scientists. 🛠️ Good first issues: Enhancing widgets, fixing bugs in layout behavior, improving examples and docs.

✅ Ideal for: Python developers who want to bridge data science and UI.

A beautifully designed, customizable component library built on Radix Primitives and Tailwind CSS. Shadcn/ui stands out for its modern patterns, typesafe components, and ease of integration with Next.js and other React frameworks. 🛠️ Good first issues: Contributing new components, enhancing documentation, fixing design inconsistencies.

✅ Ideal for: Frontend developers who love working with Tailwind and modern UI patterns.

🚀 Ready to contribute? Jump into any of these projects and start building your skills, portfolio, and community impact today!

items.title: Consultando Catálogos con la API de VuFind: un MCP en python
items.author: Horacio Degiorgi
items.content:
En el ecosistema de las bibliotecas y centros de documentación, la capacidad de acceder a múltiples catálogos de manera centralizada es fundamental. VuFind, como sistema de descubrimiento de código abierto, ofrece una potente que permite a los desarrolladores interactuar con sus datos mediante programación.

Este artículo técnico detalla el diseño y la implementación de un simple, desarrollado en Python. Este server MCP permitirá a un usuario realizar búsquedas en una o más instancias de VuFind configuradas, obteniendo los resultados directamente en su terminal.

es una interfaz de descubrimiento de recursos bibliotecarios que se instala sobre un software de catalogación tradicional (como Koha o Aleph) para ofrecer una experiencia de usuario más moderna y unificada. Su corazón, en lo que a interoperabilidad respecta, es su API.

La API de VuFind es de tipo , lo que significa que opera sobre los principios del protocolo HTTP. Las interacciones se realizan a través de URLs específicas (endpoints) y los datos se intercambian, comúnmente, en formato JSON (JavaScript Object Notation), un estándar ligero y fácil de procesar para la mayoría de los lenguajes de programación, incluido Python.

El principal endpoint que utilizaremos es . Una petición a este endpoint nos devolverá una lista de registros que coincidan con nuestros criterios de búsqueda.

Diseñando nuestro MCP en Python

El objetivo es crear un script en Python que cumpla los siguientes requisitos:

  1. Debe ser fácil agregar, quitar o modificar las instancias de VuFind a las que queremos consultar.

  2. Soporte del protocolo MCP: usando la librería FastMCP

  3. Debe aceptar una consulta de búsqueda, elegir la herramienta adecuada y realizar la consulta via api para luego devolver los resultados de manera legible.

Para esto, utilizaremos dos bibliotecas estándar y muy populares en Python:

  • : Para manejar las peticiones HTTP de forma sencilla y robusta.
  • : Para interpretar la respuesta JSON que nos devuelve la API.

Paso 1: Configuración de las Instancias de VuFind

Código Completo y Ejemplo de Uso

el código completo está alojado en github y publicado en https://mcp.so/server/vufind-mpc-api/horaciod?tab=content Y una vez instalado en claude o gemini-cli se puede hacer uso y obtener una respuesta como la de la imagen a la pregunta "en el snrd hay información o tesis de análisis de perez reverte" (esto selecciona la herramienta del mcp y realiza la llamada API para luego mostrar los registros formateados.

Video explicativo de cómo instalarlo en Gemini.

La API de VuFind abre un abanico de posibilidades para la integración y el desarrollo de herramientas personalizadas. Con unas pocas líneas de Python y la ayuda de la biblioteca , hemos construido un cliente de metabúsqueda funcional y extensible. Este tipo de desarrollos demuestra cómo la automatización y la interoperabilidad pueden potenciar enormemente el acceso a la información distribuida en los catálogos de bibliotecas, creando soluciones eficientes y adaptadas a necesidades específicas.

items.title: Day 13/100: Variable Scope – Global vs Local in Python
items.author: Rahul Gupta
items.content:
Welcome to of the series! Today, we’re diving into a super important concept: .

Have you ever defined a variable inside a function and then tried to access it outside — only to get an error? That’s a scope issue!

Understanding the difference between and variables will help you write clean, bug-free code.

  • What variable scope means
  • The difference between and variables
  • How scope affects access to variables
  • Real-world examples and best practices

determines where a variable can be accessed in your code.

In Python, there are two main types:

  • : Variables declared inside a function.
  • : Variables declared outside all functions.

A variable defined is to that function.

only exists the function. Trying to access it outside causes an error.

A variable defined all functions is and can be accessed from in the script.

⚠️ 3. Modifying Global Variables Inside Functions

You can global variables inside functions, but to them, you need to use the keyword.

This gives an error because Python treats as a new variable.

Use the keyword , as it can make debugging harder.

🎯 Real-World Example: User Sessions

🧠 Variable Resolution: LEGB Rule

Python uses the rule to resolve variable names:

  • – Local: Inside the current function
  • – Enclosing: In enclosing function(s) (for nested functions)
  • – Global: Defined at the top level of the script
  • – Built-in: Provided by Python itself (, , etc.)
  • ✅ Prefer to avoid side effects
  • ✅ Use and return values for data transfer
  • ⚠️ Avoid using unless absolutely necessary
  • 🚫 Never use the same name for local and global variables (confusing!)
  • What is and why it matters
  • The difference between and variables
  • How the keyword works
  • The rule for resolving variable names
  • Why using local scope is often safer

items.title: # Introducing Helpothon: A Platform for Developers to Make a Global Impact
items.author: Helpothon
items.content:
In a world where technology is evolving at an unprecedented pace, we often find ourselves pondering how we can leverage our skills to create a positive impact. Enter Helpothon, a platform dedicated to harnessing technology for social good. As developers, we have the unique ability to build solutions that can transform lives, bolster communities, and support businesses in their mission to make a difference.

Helpothon is a collaborative platform that connects developers with organizations looking to implement tech-driven solutions for various social challenges. Whether you're a seasoned developer or just starting your journey, Helpothon provides the tools and resources necessary to contribute meaningfully to projects aimed at helping those in need.

The platform is designed to be inclusive and accessible, encouraging individuals and teams to come together to create innovative solutions. By leveraging the collective talent of the developer community, Helpothon aims to address social issues such as education, healthcare, environment, and economic development.

Key Features of Helpothon

1. Technology for Social Good

Helpothon emphasizes the use of technology to tackle pressing social issues. Whether that's creating an application to facilitate access to education for underserved communities or developing tools that enhance local businesses, every project on the platform aims to have a tangible impact.

By providing a space where technology meets social responsibility, Helpothon empowers developers to apply their skills in ways that matter. This not only enhances your portfolio but also gives you a chance to work on projects that resonate with your values.

One of the most valuable aspects of Helpothon is its commitment to community. The platform fosters an environment where developers can connect, collaborate, and share ideas. With features like discussion forums, mentorship opportunities, and project showcases, Helpothon encourages active participation and knowledge sharing among its members.

As developers, we thrive in environments where we can exchange ideas and learn from one another. Helpothon creates that space, enabling you to build networks with like-minded individuals who are equally passionate about using technology for good.

With a diverse range of projects from various sectors, Helpothon is not limited to local or national issues; it has a global perspective. Many challenges we face today—such as climate change, poverty, and inequality—require collective action across borders. Helpothon facilitates this by enabling developers from around the world to collaborate on solutions that can scale, ultimately making a significant difference globally.

Why Developers Should Get Involved

As a developer, you have the power to drive change. By joining Helpothon, you not only enhance your technical skills but also gain experience in project management, teamwork, and problem-solving in real-world scenarios. Your contributions can lead to innovative solutions that can be implemented by organizations dedicated to making a difference.

Additionally, participating in Helpothon can open up new career opportunities. Employers increasingly value candidates who demonstrate social responsibility and the ability to contribute to meaningful projects.

Are you ready to leverage your skills for a greater purpose? Visit Helpothon to explore ongoing projects, join the community, and start making a difference today. Whether you want to volunteer your time, collaborate with others, or even start your own project, Helpothon is the perfect platform for you.

Let’s come together to use our technical expertise to create a brighter future for everyone. Your journey towards making a global impact starts now!

items.title: OrKa Cognitive Iteration Benchmark. Full Technical Report
items.author: Mak Sò
items.content:
4 July 2025OrKa Cognitive Iteration Benchmark explores whether a ‑style agent graph outperforms conventional prompt‑chaining., we forked specialised agents—logician, historian, skeptic, empathy lens—then joined their outputs through a confidence‑weighted moderator.

  • convergence (median 24.6 loops/min).
  • versus linear chains of equivalent depth.
  • Zero correlation between and —branching logic, not exotic model mix, moves the needle.

Our analysis builds on granular run traces, YAML‑defined cognition graphs, and trace analytics stored in the accompanying ZIP archive here Experiment and Data

We executed a 100‑run grid where each run tackled a distinct thematic prompt ranging from complex reasoning ("Draft a tax‑neutral spin‑off strategy") to creative synthesis ("Design a speculative Martian governance charter"). All runs used the OrKa orchestrator with deterministic memory decay and Redis‑backed trace logging.

Loops completed per minute.
total_agents_executed_all_loopsCumulative agent invocations.
Aggregate prompt + completion tokens.
OpenAI + auxiliary model fees (USD).
total_latency_ms_all_loopsWall‑clock latency in milliseconds.
Mean latency per iteration.

Each loop captures: input snapshot → agent fork → join → moderator → memory write → router decision. All intermediate payloads reside in100_run_organized/*.jsonl.

  • OrKa 0.7.0 docker compose on 16‑core AMD Ryzen, 32 GB RAM.
  • , , .
  • Qdrant 1.9 with HNSW index (m = 16, ef = 256).
  • 80 % network IO, 15 % token generation, 5 % Redis / disk.

All experiments executed under identical throttling to isolate rather than infra variance.

total_agents_executed_all_loops
total_latency_ms_all_loops

2 Inside the Orchestrator

Marvin Minsky’s 1986 thesis posits that minds emerge from swarms of specialised “agents” with no central master.

OrKa transposes this thesis into YAML‑defined

. Below we reproduce the exact graph used across all runs:

The orchestrator’s node launches four cognitive shards simultaneously. By operating under a strategy: confidence_weighted, we avoid the tail‑latency tragedies that cripple linear prompt chains.

A specialised computes a confidence vector ∈ℝ⁴ over the fork outputs, then produces a . < 0.9, the router re‑injects the consensus into memory and spins another loop, enabling self‑correction without runaway recursion.

Unlike naïve RAG that accretes context endlessly, OrKa enforces a TTL (time‑to‑live) decay on and layers. to 0.3, balancing recall against latency. Raising this to 0.5 (see §5 Recommendations) shaved 220 ms off

median loop times in confirmatory tests.

2.4 Why graphs trump chains

A linear chain with reasoning facets grows O(n) in depth; the SoM graph grows O(log n) because we join branches in constant steps.

Empirically, Figure 2 shows diminishing returns beyond ~35 agents: coordination

overhead outweighs cognitive diversity. Chains hit a similar wall at ~12 hops

but with 2–3× the latency.

Figure 1’s bimodal distribution splits runs into and. Dead‑enders correspond exactly to cases where the moderator‑confidence never exceeded 0.5—visible in trace logs asrouter_continue → false_positive_lock.

Figure 2’s scatter conveys an S‑shaped relationship: efficiency rises sharply

between 5 → 25 agents, plateaus 25 → 40, then declines. The logistic fit

() suggests coordination cost ~0.03 × agents².

Figure 3 emphasises : token spend barely budges with efficiency because our token budget is dominated by prompt scaffolding,

not loop count. In other words,

design brittle prompts, not frugal loops.

Figure 4 demolishes a popular myth: “Use an LLM zoo and you’ll outperform

GPT‑4.” All 100 runs finished with ≤ 2 models, yet delivered world‑class

performance. The winning recipe: pick high‑context model for heavy reasoning and add a for edge sanitisation.

4 Actionable Recommendations

  1. Cap fork width at 32 agents. Beyond that, cognitive overlap outweighs novel insight—see logistic inflection in Figure 2.
  2. Embed TTL‑decayed memory. OrKa’s six‑layer memory model (immediate → procedural) slashes prompt bloat by ~41 % versus sticky context windows.
  3. Introduce a timeout‑aware router. All zero‑efficiency runs lacked a ; a 20 s max‑loop timer prevents deadlocks.
  4. Temperature tuning per agent. Historian + Skeptic run best at T = 0.2; Creative Agent thrives at 0.7. Dynamic tuning saved 11 % tokens.
  5. Surfacing live enables feedback‑control throttling—see OrKa 0.7’s node.

5 Limitations & Future Work

While our 100‑run corpus spans diverse tasks, it remains synthetic. Real‑world workflows introduce shifting objectives, messy inputs, and failure modes like

auth‑token expiry. Moreover, our orchestrator assumes homogenous latency

profiles; cross‑continent deployments will need latency‑aware routing.

Future iterations will explore:

  • Hierarchical router agents that learn to compose sub‑graphs on the fly.
  • Confidence‑gated memory writes to suppress hallucination loops.
  • where agents adopt reinforcement signals instead of static prompts.

Finally, we plan to replay all runs on OrKa 0.8.0 with and compare trace‑level entropy growth.

items.title: Python Fundamentals: bandit
items.author: DevOps Fundamental
items.content:
Bandit: A Production Deep Dive into Python's Static Analysis Tool

In late 2022, a critical vulnerability was discovered in our internal data pipeline. A seemingly innocuous function responsible for deserializing configuration files from a third-party vendor was susceptible to arbitrary code execution. The root cause? A missing security check allowing the injection of malicious Python code during deserialization. While the immediate impact was contained by network segmentation, the incident highlighted a significant gap in our security tooling. We’d relied heavily on dynamic testing and code review, but lacked a robust static analysis solution integrated into our CI/CD pipeline. This led us to deeply investigate and fully adopt , a tool that has since become integral to our development workflow. This post details our journey, focusing on practical implementation, performance considerations, and the pitfalls we’ve encountered.

is a static analysis tool for finding common security issues in Python code. It’s not a linter in the traditional sense (though it overlaps), but rather a security-focused scanner. It operates by analyzing the Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) of Python code, identifying patterns known to be associated with vulnerabilities. It’s maintained by the Python Security Association and is not governed by a formal PEP, but its design aligns with Python’s emphasis on readability and maintainability. Unlike tools like , doesn’t focus on style or code quality; its sole purpose is to identify potential security flaws. It leverages a ruleset, which can be customized, to detect issues like hardcoded passwords, insecure use of , and improper input validation.

  1. API Request Handling (FastAPI): We use to scan our FastAPI endpoints for potential injection vulnerabilities. Specifically, it flags uses of or within request handlers, which are immediate red flags. This is crucial as our API processes user-supplied data.
  2. Async Job Queues (Celery/Dramatiq): Our asynchronous task queues often involve deserializing data from Redis or RabbitMQ. helps identify potential vulnerabilities in the deserialization logic, preventing malicious payloads from executing arbitrary code within our worker processes.
  3. Type-Safe Data Models (Pydantic): While Pydantic provides runtime validation, complements this by identifying potential vulnerabilities in how data is after validation. For example, it can flag insecure uses of string formatting with user-provided data, even if Pydantic has already ensured the data conforms to a specific type.
  4. Our internal CLI tools often handle sensitive configuration files. ensures these tools don’t inadvertently expose secrets or allow for command injection.
  5. Data preprocessing pipelines in our machine learning infrastructure often involve loading data from external sources. helps identify potential vulnerabilities in the data loading and transformation logic, preventing malicious data from compromising our models or systems.

Integration with Python Tooling

Our config integrates with our existing tooling:

We use a custom plugin to extend its functionality for our specific API framework (based on Requests). This plugin adds rules tailored to identify vulnerabilities specific to our API interactions. We also leverage for static type checking, and for unit and integration tests. runs as part of our pre-commit hooks and CI pipeline, failing builds if any security issues are detected. We use a custom runtime hook to dynamically load and apply the custom plugin during CI execution.

Here's an example of code flagged by and the corrected version:

flags the first example (B605 - with shell=True) because it's vulnerable to command injection. The second example avoids this by passing the command as a list, preventing shell interpretation of the user input. We favor using for data models, combined with Pydantic for validation, to ensure type safety and reduce the risk of vulnerabilities related to incorrect data handling.

Failure Scenarios & Debugging

One particularly frustrating bug involved a false positive related to a complex data transformation function. flagged it as potentially vulnerable to code injection, but after careful review, it was determined to be a false alarm. Debugging involved using to step through the code and examine the AST generated by . We also used to trace the execution flow and verify that the input data was properly sanitized. The root cause was a complex nested conditional statement that misinterpreted as a potential vulnerability. We addressed this by refactoring the code to simplify the logic and adding a more specific exclusion rule to our configuration.

Performance & Scalability

scans can be slow, especially for large codebases. We’ve found that excluding unnecessary directories (e.g., , ) significantly improves performance. We also avoid running on every commit; instead, we run it on pull requests and nightly builds. Profiling with revealed that the AST parsing phase was the most time-consuming. We’ve experimented with caching the AST to reduce redundant parsing, but the benefits were marginal. The biggest performance gain came from optimizing our custom plugin to avoid unnecessary AST traversals.

The most significant security risk associated with is relying on its output without critical review. False negatives are possible, and is not a substitute for thorough security testing and code review. Insecure deserialization remains a major concern. We enforce strict input validation and use trusted sources for configuration files. We also employ sandboxing techniques to isolate potentially vulnerable code. We've also seen issues with improper handling of sensitive data in logging statements, which can help identify.

Our testing strategy includes:

  • Verify the correctness of individual functions and modules.
  • Test the interaction between different components.
  • Property-Based Tests (Hypothesis): Generate random inputs to uncover edge cases and vulnerabilities.
  • Ensure type safety and prevent runtime errors.
  • Identify potential security flaws.

Our CI pipeline uses to run with different Python versions and configurations. GitHub Actions automatically runs on pull requests and merges. We also use pre-commit hooks to run locally before committing code.

Common Pitfalls & Anti-Patterns

  1. Ignoring Bandit Warnings: Treating warnings as mere suggestions instead of potential security flaws.
  2. Excluding entire directories or files from scans without careful consideration.
  3. Relying Solely on Bandit: Assuming that will catch all security vulnerabilities.
  4. Not Updating Bandit Regularly: Using an outdated version of with known limitations.
  5. Ignoring False Positives: Dismissing false positives without investigating the underlying code.
  6. Using overly complex suppression rules that mask real vulnerabilities.

Best Practices & Architecture

  • Embrace type hints and use to enforce type safety.
  • Design code with clear separation of concerns to reduce complexity and improve maintainability.
  • Assume that all input is malicious and validate it accordingly.
  • Break down code into small, reusable modules.
  • Use a layered configuration approach to manage different environments.
  • Use dependency injection to improve testability and reduce coupling.
  • Automate everything, from testing to deployment.
  • Ensure that builds are reproducible to prevent supply chain attacks.
  • Document code thoroughly to facilitate understanding and maintenance.

is an invaluable tool for improving the security of Python applications. While it’s not a silver bullet, it provides a crucial layer of defense against common vulnerabilities. Mastering requires understanding its limitations, integrating it into your development workflow, and continuously refining your security practices. The incident in 2022 served as a stark reminder that security is not an afterthought; it must be baked into every stage of the development lifecycle. Next steps for your team should include refactoring legacy code to address findings, measuring the performance impact of scans, writing comprehensive tests, and enforcing a linter/type gate in your CI pipeline.

items.title: PyPy: PyPy v7.3.20 release
items.content:
PyPy v7.3.20: release of python 2.7, 3.11

The PyPy team is proud to release version 7.3.20 of PyPy after the previous release on Feb 26, 2025. The release fixes some subtle bugs in ctypes and and makes PyPy3.11 compatible with an upcoming release of Cython.

The release includes two different interpreters:

  • PyPy2.7, which is an interpreter supporting the syntax and the features of Python 2.7 including the stdlib for CPython 2.7.18+ (the is for backported security updates)

  • PyPy3.11, which is an interpreter supporting the syntax and the features of Python 3.11, including the stdlib for CPython 3.11.13.

The interpreters are based on much the same codebase, thus the double release. This is a micro release, all APIs are compatible with the other 7.3 releases.

We recommend updating. You can find links to download the releases here:

We would like to thank our donors for the continued support of the PyPy project. If PyPy is not quite good enough for your needs, we are available for direct consulting work. If PyPy is helping you out, we would love to hear about it and encourage submissions to our blog via a pull request to https://github.com/pypy/pypy.org

We would also like to thank our contributors and encourage new people to join the project. PyPy has many layers and we need help with all of them: bug fixes, PyPy and RPython documentation improvements, or general help with making RPython's JIT even better.

If you are a python library maintainer and use C-extensions, please consider making a HPy / CFFI / cppyy version of your library that would be performant on PyPy. In any case, cibuildwheel supports building wheels for PyPy.

PyPy is a Python interpreter, a drop-in replacement for CPython It's fast (PyPy and CPython performance comparison) due to its integrated tracing JIT compiler.

We also welcome developers of other dynamic languages to see what RPython can do for them.

We provide binary builds for:

  • machines on most common operating systems (Linux 32/64 bits, Mac OS 64 bits, Windows 64 bits)

  • 64-bit machines running Linux () and macos ().

PyPy supports Windows 32-bit, Linux PPC64 big- and little-endian, Linux ARM 32 bit, RISC-V RV64IMAFD Linux, and s390x Linux but does not release binaries. Please reach out to us if you wish to sponsor binary releases for those platforms. Downstream packagers provide binary builds for debian, Fedora, conda, OpenBSD, FreeBSD, Gentoo, and more.

Please update, and continue to help us make pypy better.

items.title: [Personal Project 5] Spain 5–0 Portugal: All Gas in the First Half, All Brakes in the Second?
items.author: Marina Kim(Eunji)
items.content:
UEFA Women’s Euro 2025 Match: Spain Women 5 – 0 Portugal Women

Date: 3th July, 2025 | KO: 20:00 BST

Half-by-Half Key Data Comparison

  • In the first half, Spain recorded 12 total shots, 7 of which were on target (SOT), generating an impressive 2.86 expected goals (xG).
  • In the second half, they still attempted 10 shots but only managed 2 on target, with xG dropping to 1.34.
  • Portugal had no shots on target (0 SOT) in the first half and posted a mere 0.07 xG. While they improved slightly in the second half, the attacking threat remained minimal.

👉All gas in the first half, all brakes in the second. Spain exploded early, then eased off the throttle to manage the match.

  • Portugal dropped their defensive line extremely deep in the first half, registering 21 clearances and participating in 80% of all tackles. This reflects a reactive, low-block strategy that struggled under pressure.
  • But deep defending alone wasn’t enough. Spain’s tempo and pressing overwhelmed Portugal early. → Goals in the 1st and 15th minutes appeared to destabilize their defensive shape entirely.
  • In the second half, Portugal seemed to adjust their line slightly higher. Tackle involvement dropped to 54%, and clearances decreased.
  • Spain, in contrast, increased their defensive effort in the second half: tackle involvement rose from 29% to 56%. This suggests a strategic shift—lower attacking tempo, but more defensive control and pressure.

This match was a clear reminder that simply sitting deep isn’t an effective defensive strategy—at least not without structure and coordination. Portugal's ultra-defensive first-half approach allowed Spain to dictate tempo and flow, leading to defensive collapse after early goals.

Spain, on the other hand, front-loaded their energy and put the game away early, then managed the match with composure in the second half.

  • Visualization Tools: pandas, matplotlib
  • Metrics Used: TS (Total Shots), SOT (Shots on Target), xG (Expected Goals), Tackles, Clearances, Interceptions

This analysis reflects my interpretation of the data. If you have a different view, I’d be happy to hear your thoughts!

I’m always open to collaborating on creating content with football data! If this kind of analysis interests you, feel free to give me feedback. I’d love to improve my skills.

  1. pip opencv-python ultralytics numpy

🔹 Maintains a per-object path using YOLO’s tracker, enabling detection of line crossings.
